coredata.dc:description: The stability and high-dose addition of carbon dots in corrosion and scale inhibition are obstacles to their commercial application. Herein, we report a new type of functional CDs (PEI-CDA) based on Angelica residue and polyethyleneimine (PEI), which can remarkably boost the performance of corrosion and scale inhibition, while expending the application of traditional Chinese medicine waste residue. At 303.15 K, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA for carbon steel in 1.0 M HCl medium was tested by weight loss method and electrochemical method and reached over 93 %, which is significantly better than that of CDA (83 %) under the same conditions. And its corrosion inhibition efficiency can still remain stable after 15 h. The ultra-low dose (5 mg·L-1) PEI-CDA has excellent anti-corrosion protection effect on carbon steel due to its ability to form a dense and well-organized adsorption film covering on the carbon steel surface, whose adsorption includes two types of chemical and physical adsorption, and follows the Langmuir adsorption model. Furthermore, static methods display that the CaSO4 scale inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA is up to 100 % when its dosage is 100 mg·L-1. Analysis of the generated CaSO4 scale shows that the addition of PEI-CDA causes lattice distortion, chelation, and dispersion of scale. In addition, the fluorescence spectra imply that PEI-CDA can be expected to on-line detection of its residual content in the water system. The results demonstrate that PEI-CDA possesses significant potential in green inhibitors and the comprehensive utilization of waste resources.

head.ce:title: Novel functional carbon dot based on Angelica residue and polyethyleneimine: A super-efficient corrosion and scale inhibitor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The stability and high-dose addition of carbon dots in corrosion and scale inhibition are obstacles to their commercial application. Herein, we report a new type of functional CDs (PEI-CDA) based on Angelica residue and polyethyleneimine (PEI), which can remarkably boost the performance of corrosion and scale inhibition, while expending the application of traditional Chinese medicine waste residue. At 303.15 K, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA for carbon steel in 1.0 M HCl medium was tested by weight loss method and electrochemical method and reached over 93 %, which is significantly better than that of CDA (83 %) under the same conditions. And its corrosion inhibition efficiency can still remain stable after 15 h. The ultra-low dose (5 mg·L-1) PEI-CDA has excellent anti-corrosion protection effect on carbon steel due to its ability to form a dense and well-organized adsorption film covering on the carbon steel surface, whose adsorption includes two types of chemical and physical adsorption, and follows the Langmuir adsorption model. Furthermore, static methods display that the CaSO4 scale inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA is up to 100 % when its dosage is 100 mg·L-1. Analysis of the generated CaSO4 scale shows that the addition of PEI-CDA causes lattice distortion, chelation, and dispersion of scale. In addition, the fluorescence spectra imply that PEI-CDA can be expected to on-line detection of its residual content in the water system. The results demonstrate that PEI-CDA possesses significant potential in green inhibitors and the comprehensive utilization of waste resources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Q235 carbon steel acts as one of the most important materials of equipment and pipelines, which is applied in many water systems, e.g. cooling water system [1], oilfield pipeline [2], heating system [3], seawater desalination [4] and many others [5]. Corrosion and scaling issues such as CaSO4 scale, are inevitable in most water systems, which will bring significant economic consumption and production problems that cannot be ignored. Pickling is one of the necessary measures to deal with the scaling problem, and 1.0 M HCl is one of the pickling agents widely used [6]. The addition of corrosion and scale inhibitors is a simple and feasible strategy to mitigate corrosion problems associated with pickling and to prevent scaling. Some traditional corrosion inhibitors like chromate, molybdate, and phosphonate-based compounds are difficult to biodegrade and toxic, leading to serious environmental degradation [7,8]. Hence, the development of novel corrosion and scale inhibitors with nontoxic, multifunctional, and efficient is urgent.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a relatively novel zero-dimensional carbon materials with high water-solubility, low toxicity, well biocompatibility, and distinctive photoluminescence behavior [9–12]. So far, research has shown that CDs possess good corrosion and scale inhibition performance because CDs has electronegative atoms (e.g., N and O), multiple bond structures, and reactive functional groups (e.g., –COOH and –OH). For instance, Cui et al. firstly reports that the carbon steel in HCl solution exhibits remarkable corrosion prevention when exposed to CDs produced by 4-Aminosalicylic acid [13]. The CDs synthesized by citric acid has a scale inhibition efficiency against CaSO4, which is close to 100 % at its high concentration [14]. To improve performance even more, introducing one or more functional groups to prepare functionalized CDs is considered as a common modification technology [15]. To date, a series of promising functional CDs, such as N-doped CDs [16], N, S co-doped CDs [17], and CDs modified by imidazole [18] and their anticorrosion properties are investigated. The results show that these CDs own excellent adsorption and corrosion inhibition effects in the HCl environment. The unique structure of CDs endows them with the potential for corrosion and scale inhibition, and the fluorescence signal of CDs themselves is also to be advantageous to the on-line fluorescence tracing detection the concentration of scale and corrosion inhibitors [19,20]. Unfortunately, CDs has some disadvantages, such as large dosage, poor corrosion inhibition stability, and difficulty to show excellent corrosion and scale inhibition performance at the same time, all of which limit its large-scale application. Ren et al. [21] synthesized nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped CDs (N-CDs and N, S-CDs), which exhibited corrosion inhibition efficiencies of N-CDs and N, S-CDs is approximately 87.9% for N-CDs and 96.4% for N, S-CDs in 1.0 M HCl approximately at a dosage of 200 mg·L-1, respectively. Zhao et al. [22] prepared nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots, which had 97.95% in 1.0 M HCl at the concentration higher than 200 mg·L-1. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare low-dose carbon dots with high-performance. The required raw materials are not limited to chemical reagents, and biomass can also be used.

China's pharmaceutical sector has grown tremendously in recent years, which has led to bring the enormous quantities of biomass residues are generated [23,24]. At present, the methods used to dispose of leftover Chinese medicine, such as landfilling, stacking, and burning have a number of vital problems including high cost, certain environmental pollution, and squandering of resources [25]. Based on this, the Chinese medicine residues as carbon sources to synthesis the CDs not only treats the waste residues reasonably and cheaply, but also is consistent with the theme for evolution of novel corrosion and scale inhibitors.

Angelica, a ubiquitous and easily available traditional Chinese medicine, mainly contains multiple natural compounds such as polysaccharides, flavonoids, and ferulic acid [26]. After Angelica residue is carbonized under certain conditions, these CDs are easy to be functionalized on the surface, it can be speculated that they have great prospects in the fields of corrosion protection and scale inhibition. As a widely used commercial polymer, polyethyleneimine (PEI) can be combined with different substances because it contains multiple types of aminos. It has found applications in emerging frontier fields such as gas adsorption, drug delivery, and water treatment [27,28]. However, there have been no reports on the synthesis and use of composite carbon dots formed by Angelica and PEI as corrosion and scale inhibitors.

Considering the above situation, we propose a strategy for synthesizing a novel multifunctional composite carbon dots (PEI-CDA) by reacting Angelica sinensis residue with PEI. In this way, nitrogen-containing groups such as imine and carbon nitrogen structures are introduced to the surface of Angelica sinensis residue containing the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups to improve the corrosion and scale inhibition performance of PEI-CDA, and to reduce their dosage. The structure, morphology and photoelectric performance of PEI-CDA were characterized in multiple ways. Moreover, in HCl environment, the electrochemical method, weight loss method, adsorption isotherm, and kinetic calculation were used for obtaining the anti-corrosion properties of PEI-CDA, and the macro-suppression of corrosion was observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The effect of PEI-CDA on the surface of carbon steel during the inhibition process was measured by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). In addition, the scale inhibition capability of PEI-CDA towards CaSO4 was investigated through the static method, and its anti-scaling mechanism was examined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) and SEM.

Angelica bought from a local pharmacy (Xi'an, China). PEI, sodium sulfate, calcium chloride, EDTA, and potassium hydroxide were obtained by Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid and sodium chloride were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The entire trial involving water was conducted with deionized water.

The Q235 carbon steel contains the following elements (wt. %): C = 0.22, Si = 0.30, S ≤ 0.05, P ≤ 0.045, Mn = 1.40, Cr = 0.01, Ni = 0.01, and Fe, which was processed into the dimensions of 5.0 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.2 cm for weight loss tests and 5.0 cm × 1.3 cm × 0.2 cm for electrochemical tests.

The PEI-CDA was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, the waste residue obtained from boiled Angelica for 30 min was dried in an oven under 50 ℃ and smashed by a pulverizer. The mixture solution of 30 mL of deionized water, a certain mass proportion of Angelica powder and PEI (3:1, 2:1, 1:1, and 1:2) was agitated for 20 min, among which the amount of Angelica powder was fixed at 1.0 g, and then sealed in polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves and heated at a specific temperature and time. The resultant brown solution, has cooled naturally to room-temperature, underwent further filtration using qualitative filter paper and membrane filter (0.22 μm), respectively, and finally dialyzed with deionized water to obtain a pure aqueous solution of PEI-CDA. Then the PEI-CDA powder could be acquired by freeze-drying with a high yield of 55.6%. In order to compare the performance of different carbon dots, Angelica sinensis residue carbon dots (CDA) were synthesized in the same manner as described above except that PEI was not added. The whole synthesis routes of the PEI-CDA and CDA are shown in Fig. 1 .

The Shimadzu IRPrestige-21 FTIR (500–4000 cm−1) and Hitachi U-3900 UV–Vis (200–700 nm) were elected to investigate the organic structure of CDA and PEI-CDA. The size and distribution of as-prepared CDs were evaluated using JEOL JEM-2100F TEM at 200 kV. Chemical composition and bonding status of CDA and PEI-CDA were measured using ESCALAB 250Xi XPS. The fluorescence performance of PEI-CDA were observed by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and the wavelength were 300–600 nm.

Electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E) and classic three-electrode system were used for corrosion inhibition performance at 303.15 K. Q235 carbon steel was used working electrode, which has an effective area of 1 cm2, and other parts are sealed with paraffin wax. Saturated calomel electrode and platinum wire were used as reference and auxiliary electrode, respectively. Prior to each test, the working electrode was immersed into blank and 1.0 M HCl solution containing as-prepared CDs for 30 min to reach the stable open-circuit potential (OCP), and finally polarization curves tests and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were executed. EIS tests were carried out in the frequency range of 10 kHz-0.01 Hz and the amplitude of AC signal was 10 mV. The potential range of polarization curve was −0.7 V to −0.2 V with a scanning rate of 1 mV·s−1. Three parallel experiments were carried out in electrochemical tests to enhance the experiment reproducibility. Subsequently, EIS data were fitted and analyzed using ZView software.

The corrosion inhibition performance of PEI-CDA was obtained by gravimetric method. Q235 carbon steel was polished, weighed, and then immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution with different concentrations of PEI-CDA at 293.15–323.15 K. After that, the carbon steels were taken out and rinsed by deionized water and ethanol, then dried in a sealed dryer and re-weighted accurately. Corrosion rate (v corr) could be calculated by using the following equation:

present the weights (mg) of the carbon steel before and after corrosion, respectively. A is the corrosion area (cm2) of carbon steel and T is immersion time (h) of specimen in corrosive solution.

Following method was based on SY/T 5673–93 standards, the concentrations of Ca2+ and SO4 2- were about 5400 mg·L-1. The tests were carried out at 60, 70, and 80 ℃ for 25 h with a certain amount of PEI-CDA, respectively. Then the remaining Ca2+ ions were determined by the EDTA solution. The scale inhibition efficiency (η) was calculated by the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analysis of Q235 carbon steel and CaSO4 scale crystal
ce:section.ce:para: The two-dimensional and three-dimensional statuses of Q235 carbon steel surface were observed by SEM and LSCM after immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution without and with as-prepared CDs for 12 h at 30 ℃. The elemental species and content of corrosion products on steel surfaces were analyzed by EDS. CaSO4 crystals after static test without and with 10 mg·L-1 PEI-CDA were collected, repeatedly cleaned with deionized water, dried at 60 ℃, and then analyzed using XRD and SEM to investigate the scale inhibition mechanism of PEI-CDA.

The synthesis conditions are selected for subsequent study. The Supporting Information's Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 demonstrate the trend of corrosion inhibition efficiency change as a result of variations in reaction temperature, reaction duration, and PEI ratios. That is, the anti-corrosion efficiency will rise and then decreases. In total, CDA exhibits the best anti-corrosion performance when the temperature is 200 °C, and the time is 4 h. For PEI-CDA, the optimal synthesis conditions that exhibit the best anti-corrosion performance are as follows: The mass ratio of Angelica sinensis residue to PEI is 2:1, temperature is 160 °C, and time is 3 h. Lower temperature and shorter time are advantages of PEI-CDA synthesis. CDA and PEI-CDA were synthesized under the optimal conditions mentioned above for subsequent testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and fluorescence properties of PEI-CDA
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of CDA, PEI and PEI-CDA are shown in Fig. 2 a. The stretching vibration of NH produced two peaks (3281 and 1585 cm−1), and the peak of CDA at 1452 cm−1 is endowed by CN stretching vibration. Afterwards, the adsorption peaks (3403, 2939, 1654, and 1400 cm−1) can be ascribed to OH, –CH3, CO, and COO– in CDA, respectively [18]. Overall, all absorption peaks of PEI are present in PEI-CDA, but some peaks shift slightly. In terms of PEI-CDA, some appeared peaks at 2825 and 3281/1566 cm−1 are CH and NH, respectively. In addition, two new adsorption peaks located in 1513 cm−1 (–NH-CO-) and 1626 cm−1 (CN) in the spectrum of PEI-CDA, indicating that PEI has reacted with Angelica residue and fulfilled the CDA functionalized.

Fig. 2b depict the UV–vis spectra of PEI, CDA, and PEI-CDA. No absorption peak is observed in spectrum of PEI aqueous solution. The UV–Vis spectra of CDA and PEI-CDA show that weak adsorption peaks appear at 284 nm, which are owing to the n-π* electronic transition of the conjugated CO. It is particularly important that the UV–vis spectrum of PEI-CDA shows the appearance of an entirely novel absorption peak at 360 nm, whose is linked by the π-π* electronic transition from CN, suggesting that a new functional carbon dots is formed, and several nitrogen atoms are introduced into the CDs [29]. In addition, under a 365 nm UV light, the CDA and PEI-CDA solutions emit bright blue and green fluorescence, respectively, depicted in the inset of Fig. 2b. In Fig. 2c and Fig. S3, the TEM observation reveals that PEI-CDA and CDA possess regular spherical nanoparticles without apparent aggregation, mainly distributed in 2.5–5.5 nm and 3.0–6.2 nm with average particle sizes of 3.91 nm and 4.29 nm, respectively.

Next, the surface properties are further investigated by XPS. The C, N and O were detected in XPS spectra of CDA and PEI-CDA (Fig. 2d). By measurement, the CDA is composed of 74.43% C, 6.58% N and 18.98% O, while PEI-CDA is composed of 68.73% C, 17.23% N and 14.05% O. PEI-CDA contains more N elements, indicating the successful introduction of PEI. In the C element analysis, PEI-CDA contains CC/CC (284.4 eV), CN/CO (285.2 eV) and CO (287.1 eV) [30], which are proven via the three decomposed peaks (Fig. 2e). At the same time, the fitted peaks for the O1s high-resolution spectrum of PEI-CDA (Fig. 2f) which are corresponds to CO (532.5 eV), C-OH (530.4 eV), and CO (531.6 eV). Depicted in Fig. 2g are the three statuses of N element: NH (398.4 eV), CN (399.3 eV), and CN (400.7 eV).) These results indicate that the PEI is successfully combined with Angelica residue to produce functional PEI-CDA [31]. Furthermore, the addition of PEI introduces a numerous amount of the –NH, which changes the surface structure of CDA, and provides extensive active sites such as O and N (Fig. 1). This may improve corrosion and scale inhibition performance.

The PEI-CDA is dissolved in deionized water and then fluorescence properties are studied. Fig. 2h shows the optimal excitation wavelength (350 nm) and corresponding emission wavelength (464 nm) of PEI-CDA The excitation and emission spectrum are similar in shape and show mirror symmetry, indicating a great fluorescence property of PEI-CDA. At the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the correlation between the fluorescence emission intensity and the concentration of PEI-CDA is shown in Fig. 2i. The fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDA increases regularly with the rise of concentration and there is no fluorescence quenching phenomenon. In the concentration tested, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDA has an excellent linear correlation with its concentration (correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9996) (inset of Fig. 2i). PEI-CDA exhibits outstanding fluorescence quantitative measurement accurately within the dosage range, which can accomplish on-line detection of PEI-CDA in water system [32]. Furthermore, the impact of various conditions on the fluorescent intensity of PEI-CDA is examined, and the results are displayed in Fig. S4. Findings suggest that fluorescence intensity is less affected by temperature, time, and pH, and PEI-CDA possesses a stable fluorescence characteristic in the range studied.

The influence of other different factors on the fluorescence performance of PEI-CDA systematically investigated in a state of 1.0 M HCl acid simulating a corrosive environment, and detailed date in Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information. It should be noted that PEI-CDA also displays outstanding fluorescence properties, and the change of temperature and time have almost no effect on fluorescent intensity in the range studied under acidic environment. The requirements of fluorescence tracer are achieved in water systems, which means that the residual content of PEI-CDA can be detected.

The all-test solution used for corrosion properties is 1.0 M HCl, and the EIS data with varying PEI-CDA concentrations are presented in Fig. 3 a. Usually, the larger diameter of the capacitance arc represents a higher Impedance value [33]. As can be seen in Fig. 3a, the increase in PEI-CDA concentration only increased the diameter of the capacitive impedance arc, and did not change the shape of the Nyquist curve, indicating that the change in the concentration of PEI-CDA within the test range only affected the charge transfer process of the corrosion reaction, and had less influence on the corrosion inhibition mechanism of PEI-CDA [34]. The equivalent circuit model (insets of Fig. 3a and Fig. S6a) was used. Due to the non-ideal capacitive behavior of the inhomogeneous electrode, CPE dl was used to replace ideal double-layer capacitance (C dl), and the η and C dl values can be calculated by the Eq. (3) and Eq. (4),5), respectively [34–36].

where η is corrosion inhibition efficiency, R ct and R 0 ct represent the charge transfer resistance of carbon steel with and without CDs solutions, respectively. C dl is double layer capacitance, Y 0 is the modulus of CPE dl, the exponent n (−1 ≤ n ≤ 1) is associated with the distribution of time constant [36], R s is solution resistance, S represents the exposed area of carbon steel in system, d denotes double layer thickness, ε and ε 0 represent dielectric constants of double layer and vacuum.

In Table 1 , with the increase of PEI-CDA or CDA concentration, the R ct value increases. The R ct value in the presence of 5 mg·L-1 PEI-CDA is higher than that of in the presence of CDA at 30 mg·L-1. The both of them are much higher than the blank R ct value, indicating that the charge transfer process in corrosion was significantly inhibited in the presence of PEI-CDA or CDA, and the corrosion inhibitory ability of PEI-CDA is significantly stronger than that of CDA. However, with the increase of PEI-CDA or CDA concentration, the CPE dl and C dl values in Table 1 both show a decreasing trend. This is because that the water molecules adsorbed on the exterior were replaced gradually. According to Eq. (5), a lower C dl value means a thicker adsorption layer [37]. It can be clearly seen from Table 1 that when the dosage of PEI-CDA is 5 mg·L−1, C dl is the lowest. Therefore, PEI-CDA can form thicker protect layer than CDA to withstand the erosion of H+. The n values in Table 1 are always in the range of 0.7–0.9, indicating that both PEI-CDA and CDA formed on carbon steel are non-ideal capacitances with dispersion effects [37].

Fig. 3b shows that when PEI-CDA concentration increases, the impedance modulus (|Z|) at the lowest frequency grows. Generally speaking, the ability of sample to suppress corrosion increases with |Z| increasing at the lowest frequency [13]. Simultaneously, phase angle increases in the whole frequency range with the elevate of the PEI-CDA dosage (Fig. 3c), which means that the denser PEI-CDA film reduces the electron transfer produced form corrosion process, thereby alleviating the corrosion of steel [38]. Similarly, the EIS measurements of CDA also demonstrate outstanding performance (Fig. S6a–c). The improvement of corrosion resistance of PEI-CDA can be attributed to its rich lone pair of electrons provided by more hetero-atoms (N and O) whose electrons may form coordination with the vacancy of iron atoms on the steel surface, which products a more effective inhibition layer on the steel surface and is more conducive to corrosion prevention [39,40].

Fig. 3d shows that the addition of various PEI-CDA concentrations inhibits the anodic iron dissolution and the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction of iron, compared with the blank solution. In addition, the slope of PEI-CDA’s cathodic curves with different concentrations are almost the identical to those of the blank solution, indicating PEI-CDA exerts little influence on the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction mechanism. However, the shape and slope of the anode curve changed noticeably compared with CDA (Fig. S6d), suggesting that PEI-CDA affects the anodic corrosion process. And the reaction mechanism of CDA is similar to that of PEI-CDA, this also means that the adsorption essence of PEI-CDA mainly comes from CDA [16], and the corrosion inhibition performance of PEI-CDA enhanced may be attributed to the introduction of PEI.

Then the polarization curves parameters are summarized in Table 2 , and the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) is calculated by I corr according to follows [35]:
(6)

where I 0 corr and I corr denote the corrosion current densities of carbon steel without and with CDs solution, respectively.

According to Table 2, the anode Tafel slope (βa) with the addition of PEI-CDA and CDA alters distinctly more than that of the cathodic Tafel slope (βc), meaning that both CDs mainly control anode reaction of carbon steel to reduce the corrosion problem. In addition, there is no visible change in the corrosion potential (E corr) shift between uninhibited and inhibited system, which is less than 85 mV. This behavior indicates that PEI-CDA and CDA are typical mixed-type inhibitors [41]. At the same time, the I corr values decline and η values increase with the rise of concentration. In terms of concrete values, the corrosion inhibition efficiencies reach up to 94.78% and 84.91% in PEI-CDA and CDA at 5 mg·L-1, respectively, suggesting that the PEI-CDA has better anticorrosive ability than CDA and its own marvelous corrosion inhibition effect at ultra-low concentration. PEI-CDA can effectively prevents carbon steel from corroding since it is more easily adsorbed than CDA on the active locations of carbon steel [42]. This phenomenon is in accordance with the data from the EIS, revealing PEI-CDA is a high-effective corrosion inhibitor.

The corrosion inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA was further studied via corrosion rate obtained by gravimetry, and the results exhibited in Fig. 4 a. The corrosion inhibition efficiencies of PEI-CDA at 5 mg·L-1 for carbon steel are 89.7–96.4% within the 293.15–323.15 K temperature range, and its value does not change significantly, indicating that PEI-CDA has good temperature tolerance. The results obtained by the weight loss method are consistent with those obtained by the electrochemical method in Table 1. In addition, we can also observe from Fig. 4a that the corrosion rate of carbon steel in the presence of PEI-CDA is much lower than that of carbon steel in blank. The corrosion rate decreases with the PEI-CDA concentration increasing at the same temperature. The above phenomenon indicates that PEI-CDA has a significant protective effect on corrosion of carbon steel in HCl medium.

Based on the data of weight loss method, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm is carried out to provide information about the interaction between inhibitor and carbon steel surface, and the formula is as follows [38]:

where K ads represents the adsorption equilibrium constant, C is concentration of inhibitor and θ is the surface coverage (θ can be approximately replaced by η value). R represents the molar gas constant, T is the absolute temperature and 1 × 106 denotes the mass concentration of water molecules (mg·L-1).

The linear representation presented in Fig. 4b indicates the adsorption behaviors of PEI-CDA at 293.15, 303.15, 313.15, 323.15 K conform to Langmuir adsorption model. Generally, the ΔG 0 ads is be used to determine the adsorption type. If the ΔG 0 ads ≥ -20 kJ·mol−1, the adsorption type is mainly a physical adsorption process. If the ΔG 0 ads ≤ -40 kJ·mol−1, it is a chemisorption. When ΔG 0 ads ranges from −20 kJ·mol−1 to −40 kJ·mol−1, it involves both physical adsorption and physical adsorption [43,44]. The ΔG 0 ads values are −38.12, −38.22, −39.25, −41.86 kJ·mol−1, respectively, within the temperature range of 293.15–323.15 K, all around −40 kJ·mol−1. Hence, and interaction involves both physisorption and chemisorption, and chemical adsorption is predominant. Among them, chemisorption is linked to the –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 can combine with iron atoms of carbon steel, and physisorption is the electrostatic influence between the positive metal surface and the negative groups/a little Cl- absorbed on the carbon steel surface [45].

According to the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (9), the value of activation energy (E a) can be calculated by the slope in Fig. 4c. Based on transition-state theory (Eq. (10), the enthalpy of activation (ΔH) and entropy of activation (ΔS) were calculated from the slope and intercept of Fig. 4d, respectively [37]. The kinetic parameters of corrosion reactions are summarized in Table 3 .

Table 3 shows that the ΔS value are positive and increase with the increase of PEI-CDA concentration. This trend of change is attributed to the addition of PEI-CDA, which partially replaces the water molecules adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel, resulting in a more disorder in the system [46]. The ΔH value in Table 3 is greater than zero, which signifies that activation process from reactant to transition intermediate is endothermic process. The values of E a and ΔH in the presence of PEI-CDA are both higher than blank, indicating that PEI-CDA competes with the corrosion particles for the active site at this time and more heat is needed for the corrosion reaction to occur [38]. It is worth noting that the value of E a is at its maximum when the PEI-CDA concentration increases to 5 mg·L-1. A higher activation energy indicates that corrosion reaction of carbon steel is not easily carried out, which is consistent with the results of electrochemical research.

Following immersion of 12 h in different solutions, the 2D and 3D surface morphology of carbon steel is displayed in Fig. 5 . The Fig. 5a shows that the appearance of carbon steel surface is severely eroded, with more pits and cracks. Nevertheless, carbon steel exhibits a comparatively smooth surface with faint grinding traces when CDA and PEI-CDA are included (Fig. 5b,c), indicating a significant reduction in corrosion damage. Among that the steel surface shielded by PEI-CDA is the least corroded and the highest flattest. These observations show that the PEI-CDA forms a defense layer on the Q235 surface, which can potently suppress the permeation of corrosive medium into the carbon steel substrate [47]. LSCM is a precise means to explore the three-dimensional surface circumstance. It can be seen from Fig. 5d,g that the three-dimensional morphology and roughness (R a) curve of carbon steel are extremely irregular in blank HCl solution, and the R a reaches 2.520 μm. However, the surface of carbon steel becomes quite complete after the CDA and PEI-CDA are added separately, and the average roughness decreases obviously, which are reduced to 0.379 μm and 0.305 μm (Fig. 5e,f), or the reduction of 84.96% and 87.90% (Fig. 5h,i) compared to the blank solution, respectively. Meantime, PEI-CDA demonstrates remarkable anti-corrosion abilities, aligning with the findings of corrosion morphological studies.

After that, the EDS images of carbon steel are shown in Fig. 6 and C, N, Fe, and Cl elements are identified on the blank sample by EDS analysis (Fig. 6a). However, a new peak of N element appears on the carbon steel after CDA or PEI-CDA is added (Fig. 6d,h), indicating the deposition or adsorption of two kinds of CDs on the Q235 surface. Meanwhile, unlike the blank test results that the strength of Cl peak on the steel surface is reduced and Fe element is apparently increased, which confirming its inhibitory behavior. There are more Fe elements and plentiful Cl elements distributed on the surface in blank solution (Fig. 6b, c). However, the addition of two kinds of CDs presents opposite phenomenon of significant Fe elements and a small amount of Cl elements (Fig. 6e- j). In particular, PEI-CDA has a higher potential for increasing Fe element and decreasing Cl element than CDA. Meanwhile, the N element distribution on the steel surface of PEI-CDA is more uniform compared with CDA (Fig. 6g, k). These changes prove that the PEI-CDA forms a barrier layer that adsorbs onto the steel exterior to prevent the corrosion of Cl- and O2, thereby slowing down the Q235 corrosion rate [48].

The scale inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA was tested under different test conditions to evaluate its scale inhibition capability. Firstly, temperature not only restrains the solubility of CaSO4 in the pipeline but also affects the thermal stability of PEI-CDA. Therefore, the anti-scale performance of PEI-CDA, which is influenced by test temperature at its different dosages, was investigated and shown in Fig. 7 a, exhibiting that the scale inhibition efficiency gradually increases with the rising concentration of PEI-CDA. The scale inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA (100 mg·L-1) can reach 100%, 99.62%, and 91.71% at 60 °C, 70 °C and 80 °C, respectively. The anti-scaling rate varies slightly under changing temperatures, and the scale inhibition efficiency at 80 °C is relatively lower than that at 60 °C and 70 °C. On the one hand, when the temperature rises, the solubility of scale decreases due to the evaporation of water, resulting in an increase in scaling rate. On the other hand, the adsorption of PEI-CDA on CaSO4 scale is exothermic, which means that as the testing temperature increases, the adsorption capacity will decrease [49]. Generally, the temperature of most water systems is below 60 °C, and the scale inhibition efficiencies of PEI-CDA at 200 mg·L-1 is close to 100% at 60–80 °C, thus PEI-CDA owns the potential to be applied to water treatment system. The inhibiting CaSO4 scale performance by PEI-CDA may be due to the strong affinity between –COOH and Ca2+ as well as the powerful adsorption between active functional groups (–COOH, –NH2, and –NH-CO- of PEI-CDA) and CaSO4 crystal, thereby impeding the regularly growth of CaSO4 scale [32,50].

Besides, the PEI-CDA displays stable CaSO4 scale inhibition performance when PEI-CDA dosage is 100 mg·L-1 and temperature is 70 °C (Fig. 7b). When test time is as long as 50 h, the scale inhibition efficiency of CaSO4 still stays 95.22 %. The PEI-CDA has superior timeliness, and its chelating ability with Ca2+ is stronger than the combination of SO4 2- with Ca2+. Fig. 7c reveals whether the inhibitory ability of PEI-CDA on CaSO4 scales is disturbed by pH (PEI-CDA dosage is 100 mg·L-1). PEI-CDA maintains excellent scale inhibition performance when pH range is 4–10, meaning that the PEI-CDA is suitable for faintly acidic, neutral or faintly alkaline conditions. When the pH value of the solution is 2, the external functional groups cannot exist in the form of anions after the scale inhibition groups are combined with H+, which reduces the complexing ability with Ca2+ and then the scale inhibition efficiency will decrease sharply. In the meantime, there is a large amount of OH− when the pH value of the solution is 12, and Ca2+ will react with OH− to form Ca(OH)2 precipitate, which is not conducive to the anti-scaling performance. Note that the pH values of most water systems are between 6 and 9, so PEI-CDA is suitable for inhibiting CaSO4 scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and crystalline form of CaSO4 scale crystal
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM photographs was used to observe the change of CaSO4 surface morphology caused by PEI-CDA and the results are also shown in Fig. 7. The shape of CaSO4 crystal under blank condition is a regular rod-shaped with smooth surface (Fig. 7d). As Fig. 7e illustrates, at a dose of 10 mg·L-1 PEI-CDA, the regular morphology of CaSO4 crystal is destroyed and displayed as broken granular crystal with rough surface. This is because that PEI-CDA absorbs could occupy CaSO4 crystal sites, which prevents the crystal activity of calcium sulfate and destroys its structure [51]. The –COOH is capable of binding with Ca2+ and adhering to the CaSO4 microcrystals' surface, ensuring that the microcrystals have the same charge before being dispersed due to mutual repulsion [52]. Therefore, the conclusion drawn is that PEI-CDA's inhibitory process encompasses dispersion, lattice distortion, and chelation, leading to exceptional scale inhibition efficacy in CaSO4.

The XRD spectra is discussed to study the scale crystal form for CaSO4, and the results are shown in Fig. 7f. In absence of PEI-CDA, the main characteristic diffraction peaks of the gypsum crystal form (CaSO4·2H2O) appear at 11.78°, 23.48° and 28.99° in curve a. However, the intensity of characteristic peaks diminishes or completely disappears, and some different diffraction peaks like 31.56° and 57.63° are observed in the presence of PEI-CDA (Fig. 7f(b)), which correspond to the crystal form of hemihydrate gypsum (CaSO4·0.5H2O). The outcomes further confirm the ability of lattice distortion of PEI-CDA to cause changes in the morphology and structure of CaSO4 crystal, which is consistent with the SEM results.

In summary, we present a simple strategy to synthesize the corrosion and scale inhibitor by Angelica residue and PEI. Compared with CDA, synthesized PEI-CDA has more adsorption capacity, low dosage (5 mg·L-1) and higher corrosion inhibition efficiency (over 93 %) on carbon steel at 303.15 K due to the introduction of nitrogen-containing groups such as imine and carbon nitrogen structures on the surface of Angelica sinensis residue. The protective layer PEI-CDA obeys the Langmuir adsorption model, with high E a, ΔH, and ΔS values, indicating that corrosion reactions are difficult to occur. In addition, PEI-CDA owns well fluorescence stability and ability to prevent the growth of CaSO4 scale at high temperature, long time and partial acidity. Static methods display that the CaSO4 scale inhibition efficiency of PEI-CDA is up to 100% when its dosage is 100 mg·L-1. This work not only demonstrates that PEI-CDA is a promising corrosion and scale inhibitor but also that the Angelica residue used can inhibit the harm of traditional Chinese medicine waste to the environment and reduce the cost of corrosion and scale inhibition.
